key_points: The 18th-century French society saw the emergence of a Middle Class—merchants and professionals—who believed no group should be privileged by birth.
The 'Old Regime' refers to the society and institutions of France before 1789, characterized by an absolute monarchy and a rigid feudal hierarchy.
Philosophers like John Locke (Two Treatises of Government), Montesquieu (The Spirit of the Laws), and Rousseau (The Social Contract) provided the intellectual spark for the revolution.
Louis XVI was forced to call the Estates-General in May 1789 to propose new taxes; the Third Estate demanded 'one person, one vote,' which was rejected.
The Great Fear (Grande Peur) was a period of panic where peasants attacked chateaux and destroyed manorial records of dues, leading to the end of feudalism.
The Declaration of the Rights of Man and Citizen (1789) established rights such as liberty, property, security, and resistance to oppression as 'natural and inalienable'.
The Jacobin Club, led by Robespierre, represented the less prosperous sections of society and was instrumental in the downfall of the monarchy.
The Directory, a five-member executive, was established after the fall of Robespierre to prevent the concentration of power, but its political instability paved the way for Napoleon.
analytical_concepts: Separation of Powers: Montesquieu’s theory of dividing government into Legislative, Executive, and Judiciary branches.
Active vs. Passive Citizens: Under the 1791 Constitution, only men over 25 who paid taxes equivalent to 3 days of labor were 'Active' (could vote).
De-Christianization: During the Reign of Terror, churches were shut down and converted into barracks or offices.
Thermidorian Reaction: The period following Robespierre's execution, which saw the dismantling of the Terror and the rise of the wealthy middle class.
examples_and_details: Political Clubs: The Jacobins (radical) and the Cordeliers (populist) were central to spreading revolutionary ideas.
Women’s Struggle: Olympe de Gouges wrote the 'Declaration of the Rights of Woman and Female Citizen' to protest the exclusion of women from the 1791 Constitution.
The Guillotine: A device consisting of two posts and a blade, invented by Dr. Guillotin to provide a 'humane' and 'equal' method of execution.
Slave Trade: The triangular slave trade involved French merchants sailing from ports like Bordeaux and Nantes to the African coast.
exam_tips: Focus on 'Causes of the Revolution': Be ready to explain Social (Estates), Economic (Crisis), and Intellectual (Philosophers) factors.
Compare the Constitution of 1791 with the Republic of 1792; the latter abolished the monarchy entirely.
Explain how the 'Directory' failed due to internal conflicts between the Directors and the Legislative Councils.
Analyze why Napoleon was seen as a 'liberator' initially but later as an 'invading force' by conquered European nations.
Don't just list the Three Estates; explain the economic burden: the 1st and 2nd Estates owned 60% of the land despite being a tiny minority.
